title: Metals and mining outlook 2026: half-time report | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie's H1 2026 check-in confirms its January call that fragmentation would define metals markets in 2026, finding supply growth stronger than expected (pushing copper back into surplus) except in aluminium, a surprisingly contained Middle East conflict impact concentrated in specific commodities (DRI, sulphur-dependent copper/nickel), and an accelerating strategic pivot toward regionalization and electrification framed as an energy/raw-material sovereignty issue.

themes.theme: 2026 fragmentation thesis and supply growth surprise

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's January 2026 prediction that an increasingly fragmented world would define the metals and mining sector's trajectory in 2026 is playing out at the halfway point, though not exactly as originally expected.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Confirms fragmentation/geopolitics as the dominant framework for metals investors to use for the remainder of 2026, while flagging that the specific mechanisms differ from the original forecast.
insights.quote: Back in January we predicted that an increasingly fragmented world would define the trajectory of the metals and mining sector in 2026.

insights.claim: Metals supply growth in H1 2026 was stronger than Wood Mackenzie's 'growth with guardrails' expectation of cautious ongoing supply growth, even materializing in copper where surpluses are now returning to balance the market.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: A copper market moving back toward surplus is bearish for copper prices relative to the tight-supply narrative that has driven the metal's bull case; positioning should account for this swing.
insights.quote: supply build out for most metals has been stronger than we expected — even in copper, where surpluses are coming back in to balance the market
insights.locator: Supply growth continues for key metals

insights.claim: Aluminium is the exception to the broader trend of stronger-than-expected 2026 metals supply growth, with damage to Middle East facilities weakening overall aluminium supply.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Aluminium stands out as the metal most exposed to Middle East conflict-driven supply disruption, a potential relative-value long versus other base metals with resilient supply.
insights.quote: The exception is aluminium, where damage to facilities in the Middle East has weakened overall supply.
insights.locator: Supply growth continues for key metals

insights.claim: An open question for the rest of 2026 is whether long-term investment in new mining projects can continue given an environment of short-term uncertainty, despite stronger near-term supply growth.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Flags a forward risk: near-term supply strength could mask underinvestment in future capacity, setting up potential tighter markets later in the decade.
insights.quote: The question remains, however, whether long-term investments in new projects can continue in an environment beset with short-term uncertainty.
insights.locator: Supply growth continues for key metals

themes.theme: Middle East conflict's contained but uneven supply-chain impact

insights.claim: The Middle East conflict's repercussions for global metals markets have been surprisingly muted overall, with impact mainly limited to commodities and products concentrated in the region rather than spreading broadly.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Pushes back against expectations of broad-based conflict-driven commodity disruption, suggesting investors should focus risk assessment narrowly on region-concentrated supply chains rather than pricing in systemic metals-market shock.
insights.quote: Overall, the repercussions of the Middle East conflict for global markets have been surprisingly muted so far.
insights.locator: The metals supply chain has proven surprisingly resilient

insights.claim: About 32% of global direct reduced iron (DRI) production was lost to the market due to the Middle East conflict.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: A specific, material supply loss (nearly a third of global DRI) directly relevant to steelmakers and DRI-based green steel supply chains sourcing from the region.
insights.quote: about 32% of global direct reduced iron (DRI) production were lost to the market
insights.locator: The metals supply chain has proven surprisingly resilient

insights.claim: A halving of global sulphur supplies, compounded by China's sulphuric acid export ban, has affected copper and nickel production, since sulphuric acid is a key processing input for those metals.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Identifies sulphuric acid supply (driven by both conflict-related sulphur shortage and Chinese export policy) as an underappreciated input-cost/availability risk for copper and nickel producers.
insights.quote: A halving of global sulphur supplies (compounded by China’s sulphuric acid export ban) has affected copper and nickel production
insights.locator: A halving of global sulphur supplies

insights.claim: Lithium supply remains largely unscathed by the Middle East conflict, in contrast to aluminium, DRI, copper and nickel.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Lithium's insulation from the conflict differentiates its near-term supply risk profile from other battery/industrial metals affected by the region's disruption.
insights.quote: in contrast, lithium remains largely unscathed by the conflict
insights.locator: A halving of global sulphur supplies

insights.claim: A renewed Middle East conflict combined with ongoing trade restrictions are starting to put upward pressure on inflation and threaten global economic growth.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Links geopolitical conflict and trade policy directly to a macro inflation/growth risk thesis that would affect metals demand forecasts, not just supply.
insights.quote: a renewed conflict and ongoing trade restrictions are slowly starting to put upward pressure on inflation and threaten global growth
insights.locator: However, a renewed conflict and ongoing trade restrictions

insights.claim: Electrification became a major topic in Q2 2026 as disruption to oil and gas supplies shifted the framing of energy decisions from cost concerns to energy availability itself.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Suggests demand for electrification-linked metals (copper, battery materials) could be reinforced by energy-security motives rather than purely cost/decarbonization economics.
insights.quote: Electrification became a hot topic in Q2 2026 as disruption to oil and gas supplies saw cost concerns take a back seat to the question of energy availability.
insights.locator: The strategic shift towards regionalisation is accelerating

insights.claim: The trend toward economic regionalization, originally driven by a desire to bring back jobs and support decarbonization, is accelerating and increasingly framed as a sovereignty issue as geopolitical volatility rises, with greater self-sufficiency in energy, raw materials and production now seen as a strategic imperative.
insights.speaker: Peter Schmitz
insights.so_what: Reframes regionalization from an industrial-policy/jobs story into a national-security-driven structural demand shift for domestic metals supply chains — relevant to thesis on onshoring/critical-minerals investment.
insights.quote: greater self-sufficiency in energy, raw materials and production is increasingly seen as a sovereignty issue
